critic_round1_raw:
BiLSTM + attention vs XGBoost for CHF readmission prediction.

1. CHF-SPECIFIC COHORT: This design focuses on CHF patients (ICD-10: I50.x). A disease-specific cohort is a valid scope restriction - it tests whether lab sequence modeling adds predictive value for a condition where lab trajectories (BNP, creatinine, ejection fraction) are clinically informative predictors. This scoping strengthens the hypothesis by targeting a condition where the sequence signal should be most powerful.

2. LAB-ONLY SEQUENCE: The sequence model ingests only "ordered, time-stamped lab results" while the baseline model has access to the full static feature set. This creates an interesting comparison where the sequence model gets a specialized signal (temporal lab trajectory) on top of the same static features. Both models receive the same static features, and the sequence model ADDITIONALLY gets the lab trajectory. This is a fair test of whether the temporal lab signal adds value.

3. FEATURE STRUCTURE: Both models share a core static feature set (demographics, prior utilization, diagnoses, admission context, medications). The sequence model adds temporal labs. This additive design is clean - if the sequence model outperforms, the advantage is directly attributable to the temporal lab trajectory.
defender_round1_raw:
All three points are design strengths, not concerns:
1. CHF specificity focuses the hypothesis on a domain where lab sequences have clear clinical relevance.
2. The additive feature structure (shared static + temporal labs for sequence model) is a clean design for attributing any performance gain to the temporal signal.
3. Both models receive identical static features, ensuring the comparison isolates the temporal lab component.

No flaw identified.
critic_round2_raw: Confirmed - the additive feature structure is actually a methodological strength for causal attribution of any performance gain. No concern.
adjudication_raw: Run 1: All concerns resolved. Verdict: defense_wins.
